clean_story:
Falling blocks. Lines that vanish.

The name Tetris blends "tetra" (Greek for four) and "tennis"—its creator, Alexey Pajitnov, loved the sport’s back-and-forth rhythm. Like tennis, Tetris forces quick reactions, but instead of balls, you rotate seven distinct shapes made of four squares each.

The game begins with a silent challenge: blocks drop one by one, stacking like unopened mail in a crowded inbox. Your task is to arrange them into solid horizontal lines—no gaps—which then disappear, clearing space. The faster you clear, the faster they fall, until the tower touches the top. Game over.

Pajitnov wrote the first version in 1984 on an Electronika 60, a Soviet computer with 48 kilobytes of memory (a single photo today uses 3,000 times more). The screen displayed only text; blocks were brackets and dashes: `[ ][ ]` became an "L," `====` a line. Yet the core mechanic—rotating, locking, vanishing—felt instantly addictive.

Why? The brain craves order. Studies show Tetris reduces stress by occupying the mind’s "visual garbage collector," the same system that replays traumatic images. Players report seeing blocks in dreams or while blinking—a phenomenon called the Tetris Effect.

Nintendo’s 1989 Game Boy version sold 35 million copies, turning Tetris into a global handshake: a game with no language, no violence, just an endless puzzle where every piece fits somewhere.

Three related subjects are game design psychology, Soviet-era computing limits, and how repetition rewires brains.
